text: The surgeon uses an ultrasound probe to identify the intrahepatic location of the tumor and marks the resection margins. The left liver is lifted ventrally using a sponge. The hepato-duodenal ligament is dissected, allowing for the precise removal of lymph nodes and fatty tissue.

The surgeon loops two extrahepatic segment IV arteries and identifies an additional artery to segments II and III, which is precisely dissected, clipped, and cut. The same procedure is applied to the extrahepatic segment IV arteries. The left branch of the portal vein is identified, surrounded, clipped, and cut, followed by the identification, clipping, and cutting of the left hepatic bile duct extrahepatically.

Under fluorescence imaging, the demarcation line between the left and right liver is visible due to intravenous indocyanine green. The parenchymal dissection line is marked, and the left liver is mobilized from the diaphragm. A small lesion in the left hepatic vein is managed using robotic arms, with arm three compressing the vein while the other two arms place a stitch.

The surgeon continues the dissection, exposing the liver to the right side of the patient. The left hepatic vein is identified, clipped, and cut. Subsequently, the middle hepatic vein is visualized, surrounded, and looped using robotic assistance.

The surgeon begins parenchymal dissection using a harmonic scalpel after controlling the inflow and outflow of the liver. The liver capsule is incised, and coronal hemodissection proceeds from the ventral to the dorsal side. The segment five and six branch from the middle hepatic vein is clipped and cut, with major intrahepatic vessels handled similarly.

The surgeon completes the parenchymal dissection, revealing the middle hepatic vein, which is identified, clipped, and cut. The resected liver specimen is placed in a retrieval bag and extracted through a phallus sealant station. The specimen shows no capsular lesions, with a resection margin of one centimeter. Two surgical drains are placed in the operative field.
